_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Charlotte County, VA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Formed 1764 from Lunenburg. Charlotte County lies at the heart of the Virginia Southside tobacco belt, with Patrick Henry's Red Hill plantation and a deeply stratified planter-yeoman society. The county experienced significant Civil War disruption during Lee's retreat to Appomattox.

current_courthouse.location: Charlotte Court House, VA — Main Street (historic brick structure, still in use with modern additions)

previous_courthouses.location: Charlotte Court House — original courthouse on the same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current brick structure. Records transferred intact. The village that grew around the courthouse took its name from the building itself — a common Virginia Southside pattern.

record_losses.description: Charlotte County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or destruction. Records survive continuously from the county's 1764 formation. The courthouse at Charlotte Court House — one of the best-preserved antebellum courthouses in Virginia — has housed records through the Revolutionary War, the Civil War, and Reconstruction without major loss. However, Charlotte County lay directly on the line of Lee's retreat from Petersburg to Appomattox in April 1865, and the passage of both armies through the county caused disruption to civil life even though the courthouse was not targeted.
record_losses.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s records in Charlotte County are likely intact from 1764 onward. The county's records are among the most complete in Southside Virginia — a rare stroke of fortune in a region where courthouse fires destroyed the records of neighboring counties.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Lee's retreat from Petersburg to Appomattox
record_losses.description: Charlotte County was crossed by elements of both Lee's retreating army and Sheridan's pursuing cavalry in April 1865. The Battle of Sailor's Creek (April 6, 1865) occurred just north in what was then part of the county's sphere. Foraging soldiers from both armies stripped farms, burned fences, and commandeered livestock. While the courthouse was not targeted, the disruption of civil governance during 1864-1865 meant that some transactions were delayed or unrecorded.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The retreat to Appomattox passed through Charlotte County's landscape but spared its courthouse. {ancestor_name}'s legal records survived — but the family's material possessions, livestock, and stored crops may have been consumed by armies that took everything in their path.
record_losses.source: Marvel, William. Lee's Last Retreat: The Flight to Appomattox. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 2002.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from near county formation.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1764 deeds for the same territory filed under Lunenburg County.
deeds.repository: Charlotte County Clerk of Court, Charlotte Court House; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com VA land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate records survive from near county formation. Estate inventories are particularly valuable for documenting the enslaved population and material culture of the tobacco economy.
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps in bound volumes.
wills_estates.repository: Charlotte County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from near county formation.
marriages.gaps: Some early bonds incomplete. Church records supplement the official record.
marriages.repository: Charlotte County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous from near formation. Court order books document slave patrols, road maintenance orders, and the daily governance of a plantation county.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps during the Civil War period (1864-1865).
court_minutes.repository: Charlotte County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events from church records and county court orders.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for delayed certificates

church_records.completeness: Cornwall Parish vestry records survive from the county's formation. Baptist and Methodist records become significant after 1800. Charlotte County was part of the frontier of the Great Revival.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; individual church archives
church_records.online_access: Limited published transcriptions; Library of Virginia microfilm

substitute_sources.description: Charlotte County was formed from Lunenburg in 1764. All records for the Charlotte territory before 1764 — deeds, wills, court orders, tithable lists — are filed under Lunenburg County.
substitute_sources.repository: Lunenburg County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Charlotte County family that predates 1764. The Lunenburg records also cover the territory that became Mecklenburg and Halifax — researchers must check Lunenburg for the earliest generation of Southside families.
substitute_sources.source: Bell, Landon C. Sunlight on the Southside: Lists of Tithes, Lunenburg County, Virginia, 1748-1783. Philadelphia: George S. Ferguson Company, 1931.

substitute_sources.source_type: Patrick Henry estate and family records
substitute_sources.description: Patrick Henry's Red Hill plantation was in Charlotte County, and the Patrick Henry Memorial Foundation maintains archival collections related to Henry and the families who interacted with him — neighbors, business partners, and the enslaved people held at Red Hill.
substitute_sources.repository: Red Hill, Patrick Henry National Memorial, Brookneal, VA; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For families in the immediate vicinity of Red Hill, the Henry archives may contain references to neighbors, land transactions, and enslaved people that supplement the county records.
substitute_sources.source: Mayer, Henry. A Son of Thunder: Patrick Henry and the American Republic. New York: Franklin Watts, 1986.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Charlotte County enumerated from the first federal census. Slave schedules (1850-1860) are critical for a county where enslaved people constituted approximately half the population.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1790 census for Charlotte County documents the post-Revolutionary War planter class and the beginnings of westward migration from the exhausted tobacco lands.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Charlotte County, VA.

narrative_hooks: Charlotte County's courthouse has survived intact since 1764 — one of the most complete record repositories in Southside Virginia. If {ancestor_name} lived here, the paper trail from deed to death is likely unbroken.

Before 1764, Charlotte was part of Lunenburg — the 'mother county' of the Southside — and {ancestor_name}'s earliest records may be filed under Lunenburg, a jurisdictional shift that separates the first and second generation of the same family into different county clerks' offices.

Lee's retreating army passed through Charlotte County in April 1865, and soldiers from both sides stripped the farms of everything that could be eaten or burned — {ancestor_name}'s legal records survived in the courthouse, but the livestock, the fencing, and the stored crop did not.
Patrick Henry lived and died in Charlotte County, and if {ancestor_name} was a neighbor, a debtor, or an enslaved person at Red Hill, the Henry family papers may hold records the courthouse does not.

Charlotte County's estate inventories are among the most detailed in the Southside — room-by-room lists of household goods, livestock, and enslaved people that document not just what {ancestor_name} owned, but how {pronoun_subject} lived.
source_references: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Bell, Landon C. Sunlight on the Southside: Lists of Tithes, Lunenburg County, Virginia, 1748-1783. Philadelphia: George S. Ferguson Company, 1931.
Marvel, William. Lee's Last Retreat: The Flight to Appomattox. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2002.
Mayer, Henry. A Son of Thunder: Patrick Henry and the American Republic. New York: Franklin Watts, 1986.
